content.text: Agentic AI systems are harder to reason about upfront than conventional software. The interaction between a prompt, a model, and a set of tools produces behavior that surprises even experienced engineers. Rapid prototyping — structured, time-boxed experiments that generate evidence before committing to an implementation — is how skilled AI engineers avoid expensive dead ends and discover what actually works.
What it is

Prototyping and rapid iteration in agentic AI is the practice of building the smallest possible working version of a capability — a spike — to test a specific hypothesis about model behavior, tool integration, or user interaction, then using the evidence from that spike to make a concrete decision: persevere, pivot, or discard. It is not unstructured hacking; each prototype has an explicit goal and a defined success criterion evaluated before the next iteration begins.

A spike differs from production code in two important ways: it is deliberately scoped to test one thing, and it is disposable by default. The code lives in an /experiments directory or a throwaway branch. Nothing from a spike is merged to main until a decision gate confirms that the approach has sufficient evidence to invest in hardening. This separation is not bureaucratic overhead — it is the mechanism that keeps the rest of the codebase clean while exploration is happening.

Rapid iteration means completing the hypothesis → build → evaluate → decide loop as fast as possible — targeting hours to one or two days per cycle. The speed is not about cutting corners; it is about spending as little time as possible on ideas that will not survive contact with real model output, real latency, or real users.
Why it matters

The fundamental challenge of building agentic systems is that model behavior is not predictable from first principles. A tool-calling architecture that sounds compelling in a design doc may produce agent loops, hallucinated tool arguments, or context overflow in practice. Discovering this after three weeks of engineering investment is far more expensive than discovering it after a one-day spike.

Rapid prototyping is also how engineers calibrate their intuitions about AI systems. New engineers often overestimate what can be accomplished with a complex prompt and underestimate what a well-structured tool call can do. The only way to develop accurate intuitions is to run enough experiments to build a personal evidence base about what works, what fails, and why.

Organizationally, visible prototyping practice protects teams from pressure to ship prematurely. When stakeholders can see that a team is generating evidence systematically — with explicit decision gates — rather than indefinitely "exploring," prototyping becomes a credible investment rather than an excuse for slow delivery.
Benefits
Kills bad ideas cheaply: a one-day spike that proves an approach does not work prevents weeks of engineering effort on a dead end.
Generates concrete evidence: decisions are grounded in actual model outputs rather than intuition or analogies to prior systems.
Builds a reusable eval foundation: each prototype that reaches the evaluate phase produces test cases and expected behaviors that become the eval corpus for production.
Keeps the team unstuck: when engineers are uncertain about the right approach, a two-day spike resolves the uncertainty faster than discussion alone.
Creates stakeholder confidence: a working demo — even a rough prototype — is far more persuasive for buy-in than a design document.
Trade-offs
API cost: running eval loops against a production model has real token costs; teams without cost tracking can burn budget quickly in heavy prototype phases.
Prototype debt: spikes that are not deleted after a decision gate accumulate as confusing dead code; discipline around cleanup is required.
Premature optimization pressure: stakeholders who see a working prototype often want to ship it without hardening; teams must maintain the distinction between prototype and production-ready.
Signal quality: a poorly designed spike may generate misleading evidence; if the test inputs are unrepresentative of production, the results will not predict production behavior.
How it works
The prototyping cycle has four phases that repeat until the team reaches a final decision.
Phase 1: Define the hypothesis

Write a single sentence in the form "We believe that [approach X] will [produce outcome Y] because [reason Z], and we will know this is true if [measurable signal S]." The hypothesis must be falsifiable within the spike's time budget. Without an explicit hypothesis, a spike has no way to succeed or fail — it just runs until time is up.
Phase 2: Build the spike

A spike is the smallest amount of code needed to test the hypothesis. It typically consists of a prompt, a minimal tool harness, a test input set (10-30 representative examples), and a script that runs the inputs and logs outputs. Error handling, logging infrastructure, and code quality are deliberately minimal — the spike will be deleted or rewritten. The engineering investment is in the correctness of the test design, not the quality of the implementation.
Phase 3: Evaluate outputs

Run the spike against the test inputs and score outputs against the success criterion defined in the hypothesis. Evaluation has two components: automated scoring (exact match, rubric-based LLM judge, or regex patterns) and human review of a sample of outputs. The automated score gives a number; the human review catches failure modes the automated scorer misses, such as technically correct but uselessly verbose responses, or outputs that satisfy the criterion via unintended reasoning.
Phase 4: Decision gate

Every spike ends with an explicit decision: persevere (evidence supports the approach; begin hardening), pivot (the core idea has merit but the implementation needs to change; start a new spike), or stop (the approach does not work; kill it and move on). This gate is not optional. A spike without a decision is a cycle of exploration without learning.

The decision gate has a second function beyond choosing the next action: it documents the evidence. A one-paragraph write-up of what the spike found — pass rate, failure patterns, unexpected behavior, cost per call — serves as organizational memory. Six months later, when someone asks "why didn't we build it the obvious way?" the spike record provides the answer. This documentation cost is 15 minutes; the knowledge it preserves is significant.
Iteration across spikes

Most capabilities require two to four spikes before reaching a persevere decision. Each pivot generates a new hypothesis that incorporates what the previous spike revealed. A first spike might test whether the model can perform a task at all; a second spike tests whether the latency is acceptable; a third tests whether the approach handles edge cases in the production input distribution. This progression from feasibility to performance to robustness is the natural arc of structured prototyping.

The total elapsed calendar time for two to four spikes, each one to three days, is one to two weeks. This is faster than the typical outcome of unstructured exploration, which may run for months without producing a decision. The structure — hypothesis, time box, explicit gate — is what compresses the timeline.
Architecture

attrs.alt: Four-phase prototyping cycle: Define Hypothesis → Build Spike → Evaluate Output → Decision Gate with three exit paths: Persevere (harden), Pivot (new hypothesis), Stop (kill prototype)

content.text: The diagram shows the four-phase iteration cycle with explicit decision exits. Spikes are time-boxed and disposable by design. The eval phase drives the decision gate using both automated scoring and human review, ensuring that neither intuition alone nor metric-gaming determines the outcome.
When to use it

Rapid prototyping applies whenever a team faces a decision about model behavior that cannot be reliably predicted from documentation or prior experience alone. Specifically: when starting a new agent capability with no prior art in the codebase; when choosing between two plausible prompt architectures; when integrating a tool whose behavior under real inputs is unknown; when a production issue reveals a prompt failure mode whose fix is non-obvious; and when evaluating a new model version before migrating a production workload.

Prototyping is not the right tool for well-understood changes — adding a field to a structured extraction schema, extending a working tool definition, or updating few-shot examples. These changes are safe to implement directly and validate via the existing eval suite. The decision to spike versus implement directly is a judgment call about prediction uncertainty: high uncertainty warrants a spike; low uncertainty warrants a direct implementation.
How to implement

Create an /experiments directory in the repository root. All spike code goes here. Nothing in /experiments is expected to meet production code quality standards; this boundary must be explicit in the CONTRIBUTING guide.
For each spike, create a subdirectory with a README.md containing: the hypothesis text, the success criterion, the time budget, and — when done — the decision and the evidence that supported it.

Build a reusable spike harness: a thin Python script that takes a prompt file, a test input JSONL, and an output directory, calls the model API, and writes outputs as JSONL with timestamps and token counts. Reuse this harness across spikes to minimize setup time.

Build a lightweight LLM-as-judge scorer that takes the output JSONL and a rubric, calls a strong model (e.g., Claude 3.7 Sonnet) to score each output, and produces a pass rate. This is the automated eval signal.
Run the spike, apply the scorer, then manually review 10-20 outputs — especially failures and edge-passing cases — before making the decision gate call.

After the decision gate, either delete the spike directory (stop), start a new spike with the revised hypothesis (pivot), or promote the test inputs to the production eval corpus and begin hardening the implementation (persevere). Never leave a spike in limbo.
Examples
Spike harness (Python)

# experiments/spike_runner.py
import json, time, anthropic
from pathlib import Path

def run_spike(prompt_path: str, inputs_path: str, output_path: str, model: str = "claude-sonnet-4-5"):
    client = anthropic.Anthropic()
    system_prompt = Path(prompt_path).read_text()
    results = []

    with open(inputs_path) as f:
        inputs = [json.loads(line) for line in f]

    for i, inp in enumerate(inputs):
        start = time.time()
        msg = client.messages.create(
            model=model,
            max_tokens=1024,
            system=system_prompt,
            messages=[{"role": "user", "content": inp["input"]}],
        )
        elapsed = time.time() - start
        results.append({
            "id": i,
            "input": inp["input"],
            "expected": inp.get("expected"),
            "output": msg.content[0].text,
            "input_tokens": msg.usage.input_tokens,
            "output_tokens": msg.usage.output_tokens,
            "latency_s": round(elapsed, 2),
        })
        print(f"[{i+1}/{len(inputs)}] {elapsed:.1f}s")

    Path(output_path).write_text("\n".join(json.dumps(r) for r in results))
    print(f"Done. {len(results)} outputs written to {output_path}")

if __name__ == "__main__":
    import sys
    run_spike(sys.argv[1], sys.argv[2], sys.argv[3])
Async parallel spike runner

# experiments/async_spike_runner.py
# Use when the test set is large and serial calls are too slow
import asyncio, json, time, anthropic
from pathlib import Path

async def run_single(client, system_prompt, inp, model, sem):
    async with sem:
        start = time.time()
        msg = await client.messages.create(
            model=model,
            max_tokens=1024,
            system=system_prompt,
            messages=[{"role": "user", "content": inp["input"]}],
        )
        return {
            "id": inp["id"],
            "input": inp["input"],
            "expected": inp.get("expected"),
            "output": msg.content[0].text,
            "input_tokens": msg.usage.input_tokens,
            "output_tokens": msg.usage.output_tokens,
            "latency_s": round(time.time() - start, 2),
        }

async def run_spike_async(prompt_path, inputs_path, output_path,
                          model="claude-sonnet-4-5", concurrency=10):
    client = anthropic.AsyncAnthropic()
    system_prompt = Path(prompt_path).read_text()
    sem = asyncio.Semaphore(concurrency)

    with open(inputs_path) as f:
        inputs = [json.loads(line) for line in f]

    tasks = [run_single(client, system_prompt, inp, model, sem) for inp in inputs]
    results = await asyncio.gather(*tasks)

    Path(output_path).write_text("\n".join(json.dumps(r) for r in results))
    total_tokens = sum(r["input_tokens"] + r["output_tokens"] for r in results)
    print(f"{len(results)} outputs. Total tokens: {total_tokens:,}")

if __name__ == "__main__":
    import sys
    asyncio.run(run_spike_async(sys.argv[1], sys.argv[2], sys.argv[3]))
LLM-as-judge scorer

# experiments/score_outputs.py
import json, anthropic
from pathlib import Path

RUBRIC = """
Score the following AI output on a scale of 0-3:
0 = Wrong or harmful
1 = Partially correct but missing key elements
2 = Correct with minor issues
3 = Correct, concise, and complete

Respond with JSON: {"score": <int>, "reason": "<one sentence>"}
"""

def score_outputs(outputs_path: str, rubric: str = RUBRIC):
    client = anthropic.Anthropic()
    scores = []

    with open(outputs_path) as f:
        outputs = [json.loads(line) for line in f]

    for out in outputs:
        prompt = f"Input: {out['input']}\nOutput: {out['output']}"
        msg = client.messages.create(
            model="claude-sonnet-4-5",
            max_tokens=128,
            system=rubric,
            messages=[{"role": "user", "content": prompt}],
        )
        result = json.loads(msg.content[0].text)
        scores.append({"id": out["id"], **result})

    pass_rate = sum(1 for s in scores if s["score"] >= 2) / len(scores)
    print(f"Pass rate (score >= 2): {pass_rate:.1%} ({len(scores)} outputs)")
    return scores, pass_rate
Real-world example

A team building a document summarization agent needed to decide between two approaches: a single-pass summarizer that processed the full document in one call, and a hierarchical summarizer that chunked the document, summarized each chunk, then synthesized a final summary. The hypothesis for the first spike was: "A single-pass summarizer will achieve >= 80% quality scores on our 50-document test set with documents up to 40,000 tokens." The spike ran in four hours. The pass rate was 61% — largely because documents over 20,000 tokens produced summaries that skipped the second half. Decision: pivot. The second spike tested the hierarchical approach. Pass rate: 84%. Decision: persevere. The team then hardened the hierarchical implementation, promoted the 50-document test set to the production eval corpus, and shipped two weeks later. Total prototype time: two days. Total decisions made from evidence rather than opinion: two.
Common mistakes
Vague hypotheses: "Let's see how the model handles this" is not a hypothesis. Without a falsifiable success criterion, there is no decision gate — just open-ended exploration.
Overfitting the spike: cherry-picking test inputs that make the prototype look good rather than selecting inputs that are representative of the production distribution.
Not deleting failed spikes: experiments that did not persevere should be deleted or archived. Leaving them in the repository creates confusion about what the team actually builds.
Shipping prototypes directly: a spike that achieves the success criterion is not production-ready. It needs error handling, cost monitoring, logging, and security review before deployment.
Skipping human review: automated scores can miss qualitative failures — responses that technically satisfy the rubric but are unhelpful or subtly wrong in ways a human catches immediately.
Best practices
Set a hard time box: decide before starting how many days the spike has. When the time box expires, evaluate with whatever evidence exists. Unlimited spikes create unlimited schedules.
Use real or realistic inputs: the test set should reflect the actual distribution of inputs the system will receive in production. Synthetic inputs designed to be easy understate the real challenge.

Track costs per spike: log token usage and compute the API cost for each spike run. This builds intuition about relative costs of different architectures and prevents prototype phases from consuming the team's AI budget.
Promote test inputs to the eval corpus: the representative inputs gathered during prototyping are the highest-quality additions to the production eval suite. Capture them systematically.

Share spike findings: a brief written summary of what the spike found — even for failed approaches — is valuable organizational knowledge. Future engineers facing similar decisions will benefit from knowing what was tried.
Performance and scalability

Prototype phase performance expectations differ from production requirements. A spike running 50 test inputs serially in Python is fine — the goal is signal quality, not throughput. Parallelizing spike runs with asyncio can reduce wall-clock time by 5-10x for large test sets, which matters when iterating on the same spike multiple times in a single day.

When a spike succeeds and the team begins hardening, performance testing becomes a separate explicit phase. The first performance question to answer is: what is the p95 latency of the agent under realistic load? This is not the spike's job — it is the job of a dedicated load test run against the hardened implementation before it reaches staging.
Tooling and ecosystem
Anthropic SDK (Python or TypeScript): primary interface for calling Claude models in spikes. Use the Messages API directly in spikes rather than adding framework abstractions.
Jupyter notebooks: useful for interactive eval review — load the output JSONL, display random samples, and annotate failures inline. Not appropriate for production agent code.

Braintrust / LangSmith / PromptLayer: eval platforms that provide a UI for reviewing outputs, tracking scores across spike iterations, and managing test datasets. Useful when the team runs many spikes per week.
tiktoken / Anthropic token counter: for estimating token counts before running a spike, to avoid unexpected costs from oversized prompts.
Comparison with alternatives

Design-then-build without prototyping: teams write detailed design documents and then implement the full agent system before testing model behavior. This works well for conventional software where behavior is fully predictable from specifications; it fails for agentic AI because model behavior is only observable, not derivable. Design-then-build for AI systems frequently produces weeks of effort that must be partially discarded after the first real eval run.

Continuous exploration without decision gates: some teams run ongoing experiments without structured hypotheses or decision gates, hoping good ideas will emerge. This produces activity but rarely produces learning. Without a defined success criterion, no experiment can produce a clear decision signal. Structured rapid prototyping converts exploration into evidence-driven decisions.
Security and reliability

Prototypes that reach production readiness must undergo a security review that the spike deliberately skipped. Specifically: prompt injection vectors must be tested (can adversarial user inputs hijack tool calls?), credential handling must be reviewed (API keys must not appear in spike code committed to the repository), and output sanitization must be verified (can the model produce outputs that cause XSS or injection attacks downstream?).

The /experiments directory should be excluded from the credential scanner's normal reporting scope — developers routinely commit test API keys in spikes — but it must be clearly documented that spike code cannot be promoted to production without a full security review and credential rotation. A pre-commit hook that warns (not blocks) when a credentials pattern is detected in /experiments strikes the right balance between speed and safety during prototyping.
Advanced iteration patterns
Parallel spiking

When two plausible approaches exist and neither has clear theoretical superiority, run them in parallel rather than sequentially. Assign one engineer to each spike for two days, share the same test input set, and compare pass rates at a joint decision gate. Parallel spiking doubles the engineering cost but halves the calendar time to a decision. It is most valuable when the team has strong disagreement about which approach is better — instead of debating, both hypotheses get tested simultaneously.
Adversarial test sets

A spike that only tests on easy inputs produces a misleading pass rate. After the initial spike run, deliberately include adversarial inputs: edge cases that resemble production input patterns but are harder, inputs that previously caused failures in similar systems, and inputs designed to trigger the specific failure modes the hypothesis is concerned about. An approach that passes 90% of representative inputs but fails 60% of adversarial inputs is not production-ready.
Prompt iteration within a spike

A single spike may run the same test set against two or three prompt variants to find the best phrasing before the decision gate. This is prompt iteration, not spike iteration — the hypothesis stays constant, the prompt wording changes. Keep a structured log of each prompt variant tried within the spike: what changed, what the pass rate was, and what failure patterns remained. Without this log, the team cannot reconstruct which prompt they are running or why it differs from the last version.
Mental model

Think of each spike as a scientific experiment run under time pressure. A scientist does not prove that an approach works by arguing for it — they design an experiment, run it, and let the data decide. The hypothesis is the experimental design; the spike is the experiment; the eval is the measurement; the decision gate is the conclusion. Experiments that fail to support the hypothesis are not failures of the team — they are successful experiments that eliminated a wrong path. The team that kills three bad ideas in a week via disciplined spiking is making faster progress than the team that spent the same week implementing the first idea that sounded plausible.
Key takeaways
Every spike needs an explicit falsifiable hypothesis with a measurable success criterion before code is written.
Spikes are disposable by design: they live in /experiments and are deleted or archived after the decision gate, not evolved into production systems.
Every spike ends with a decision: persevere, pivot, or stop. No decision means the spike failed its purpose regardless of what the code does.
Test inputs gathered during prototyping are the highest-quality additions to the production eval corpus — promote them systematically.
Velocity of learning — how fast the team kills bad ideas and validates good ones — is more important than velocity of shipping during the prototyping phase.
